name: Fasold
description: Fasold, also known as Fafnir's father, is a complex figure in Germanic mythology, appearing prominently in the Volsunga Saga and related narratives. He is a giant, often described as greedy and powerful, whose actions have significant repercussions for the hero Sigurd and the Volsung clan. Fasold, along with his brothers Regin and Otr, demands wergild (man-price) from the gods Odin, Hoenir, and Loki after Loki kills Otr, who was in the form of an otter. The wergild is paid in gold, including the cursed treasure of Andvari. Fasold’s greed leads him to kill his own father, Hreidmarr, to possess all the gold, including the ring Andvaranaut, which carries a curse of destruction and misfortune. He then transforms himself into a dragon to guard his hoard, becoming the fearsome dragon Fafnir. Fasold’s transformation and guarding of the hoard ultimately set the stage for Sigurd's famous dragon-slaying. While technically not a hero in the conventional sense, Fasold's story is crucial to the heroic narrative of Sigurd, as his actions create the challenge that Sigurd must overcome. His avarice and treachery serve as a cautionary tale about the corrupting influence of wealth and the dangers of unchecked ambition. Fasold's transformation into a dragon and his eventual death at Sigurd's hand are pivotal moments in Germanic mythology, shaping the destinies of heroes and gods alike. He represents the destructive force of greed and the inevitable consequences of violating sacred laws and familial bonds. Fasold's legacy lies in the unfolding drama of the Volsung saga and the consequences of his fatal decisions.
subtitle: Greedy Giant, Dragon Hoarder, Volsung Foe
achievements: Served as an antagonist for Sigurd

companions.name: Regin
companions.description: Regin is Fasold's brother and acts as a mentor and smith for Sigurd, ultimately manipulating Sigurd to slay Fasold/Fafnir for the treasure. He is motivated by greed and revenge against his brother.

companions.name: Otr
companions.description: Otr is Fasold's other brother who could transform into an otter. His death by Loki's hand initiates the chain of events leading to Fasold's acquisition of Andvari's hoard and subsequent curse.

companions.name: Sigurd
companions.description: Sigurd is trained and armed by Regin. He is the hero who ultimately slays Fasold (in dragon form) on Regin's behalf, though he later becomes wise to Regin's treachery.

companions.name: Andvari
companions.description: While not willingly, Andvari is related to Fasold through the curse of his ring, which brings misfortune upon whoever possesses it, including Fasold. Andvari acts as a cursed treasure.

keyMyths.title: The Otter's Ransom
keyMyths.description: Loki kills Otr, who is in otter form. Fasold and his brothers demand a ransom of gold to cover the otter's skin, which the gods must fill and cover with gold.

keyMyths.title: The Slaying of Hreidmarr
keyMyths.description: Fasold, driven by greed for the cursed gold, murders his father, Hreidmarr, to claim the entire treasure for himself, initiating the cycle of violence and tragedy.

keyMyths.title: Transformation into Fafnir
keyMyths.description: To protect his ill-gotten gains, Fasold transforms himself into a fearsome dragon, becoming the dragon Fafnir, guarding the treasure in the Gnita-Heath.

sources.author: Anonymous
sources.description: A primary source for the story of Fasold and the Volsung clan.
sources.text: A primary source for the story of Fasold and the Volsung clan.

sources.author: Snorri Sturluson
sources.description: Contains references and allusions to the Volsung saga and related myths.
sources.text: Contains references and allusions to the Volsung saga and related myths.

worshipPractices.name: Offerings to Giants
worshipPractices.description: While not directly worshipped, giants like Fasold received offerings, often of food or crafted goods, to appease their potential wrath. These offerings were less about devotion and more about ensuring the safety and prosperity of the community by preventing giants from causing harm. The offerings were typically left at the edges of settlements or in places considered the giants' domain.

worshipPractices.name: Honoring Strength and Craftiness
worshipPractices.description: Fasold, despite his negative traits, represented immense strength and cunning. While direct worship was unlikely, qualities like strength, resourcefulness, and the ability to hoard wealth were respected. Stories of his deeds served as cautionary tales, but also examples of ambition and the power that comes with accumulating resources.

festivals.name: None Directly Associated
festivals.description: No known festivals are directly associated with Fasold. His role as a figure of greed and destruction makes him an unlikely candidate for celebratory festivals. However, his story may have been told during winter nights as a cautionary tale.

cultural.modernLegacy: Fasold, primarily through the Fafnir character he becomes after killing his father for gold, continues to inspire portrayals of greed and its corrupting influence in modern fantasy literature and games. He serves as a model for powerful, avaricious creatures that heroes must overcome, embodying the dangers of unchecked ambition. His story also contributes to broader themes of family betrayal and the consequences of obsession with wealth.

cross_cultural_parallels.similarity: Both Fasold and Typhon are monstrous beings associated with immense power and greed, representing a primal, chaotic force. They both possess a dangerous, destructive nature that challenges the established order.

Hiranyakashipu, like Fasold, is driven by an insatiable desire for power and control. Both are formidable figures who challenge the gods and established cosmic order, creating conflict and requiring a heroic intervention to restore balance.

Apep is a serpent of chaos, embodying negativity and a threat to the sun god Ra's journey. Similar to Fasold's greed disrupting the natural order, Apep represents a constant struggle against positive forces and stability.

associations.name: Dragon
associations.significance: Fasold's transformation into a dragon to guard his treasure connects him to greed and hoarding.

associations.name: Gold
associations.significance: Fasold's insatiable desire for gold represents his materialistic nature and ultimate downfall.

associations.name: Greed
associations.significance: Fasold embodies greed as the driving force behind his actions and tragic fate.

associations.name: Seven
associations.significance: Seven, representing completeness and a cycle, highlights the completeness of his greed, which leads to a cycle of misery.

associations.name: Stone
associations.significance: Stone symbolizes Fasold's stubbornness and unyielding nature in his pursuit of wealth.

associations.name: Rust
associations.significance: Rust embodies the decay and corruption that Fasold's greed brings upon himself and his family.

associations.name: Transformation
associations.significance: Fasold's transformation into a dragon shows how greed can transform and corrupt an individual.

quests.title: The Price of Brotherhood
quests.description: Fasold, along with his brothers Regin and Otr, is tasked with building a house for the gods. He demands a hefty price for his labor, causing tension and conflict among the brothers, ultimately leading to Otr's tragic demise.
quests.outcome: The house is built, but the seeds of greed and betrayal are sown within the family, leading to future tragedies.

quests.title: The Andvari's Curse
quests.description: After Otr is killed by Loki, Fasold demands wergild (man-price) equal to covering Otr's skin in gold. Loki obtains this gold, including the ring Andvaranaut, from the dwarf Andvari, who curses the treasure to bring misfortune to its possessor.
quests.outcome: Fasold receives the gold, unwittingly accepting a curse that will plague his family and lead to his own death.

quests.title: Guarding the Treasure
quests.description: Driven by greed, Fasold slays his father to claim the entire treasure hoard for himself. He then transforms into a dragon, Fafnir, to better protect his ill-gotten gains.
quests.outcome: Fasold, now Fafnir, becomes a fearsome dragon guarding the treasure, his humanity lost to avarice.

quests.title: The Dragon's Hoard
quests.description: Fasold as Fafnir has claimed the hoard of Andvari, and now needs to find a safe place to hide it. He must overcome traps and riddles to keep his treasure safe from others looking to obtain it.
quests.outcome: Fasold successfully hides the treasure, becoming a dragon and lying on the hoard, awaiting intruders.

feats.title: Construction of the Gods' Dwelling
feats.description: Fasold, along with his brothers, single-handedly constructs a magnificent dwelling for the gods as a service or payment. This task demonstrates his immense strength and architectural skill.
feats.significance: The dwelling provides a safe haven for the gods and showcases Fasold's capabilities, highlighting the power of the giants.

feats.title: Claiming the Andvari's Hoard
feats.description: Fasold successfully demands and receives a massive amount of gold as wergild for his son's death. This feat demonstrates his power and influence, as well as his ruthless determination.
feats.significance: The acquisition of the hoard marks a turning point in Fasold's life, fueling his greed and setting the stage for future tragedies.

feats.title: Transformation into Fafnir
feats.description: Driven by avarice, Fasold undergoes a physical transformation into a fearsome dragon, Fafnir. This act signifies his complete descent into greed and the loss of his humanity.
feats.significance: The transformation into Fafnir represents the corrupting influence of wealth and the destructive power of unchecked desire.

feats.title: Slaying of Hreidmar
feats.description: Fasold slays his own father, Hreidmar, in order to claim the entire treasure hoard of Andvari for himself. This act highlights his avarice and willingness to commit the ultimate betrayal.
feats.significance: This act demonstrates the power of greed and sets him on the path to becoming Fafnir.

extendedContent.title: Early Life & Transformation: From Giant to Dragon
extendedContent.content:
Fasold, initially a giant of considerable strength and cunning, resided with his brothers Regin and Otr. Their lineage is often linked to Hreiðmarr, a powerful figure associated with wealth and magical artifacts. The early life of Fasold is largely obscured, but the narrative focuses on the tragic event that fundamentally shapes his destiny: the accidental killing of his brother Otr by Loki. Otr, possessing the ability to transform into an otter, was slain while fishing, and the Aesir were compelled to pay a substantial wergild to Hreiðmarr, Fasold, and Regin, filling Otr's otter skin with gold and covering it entirely.

This payment included Andvari's Ring and the cursed gold hoard, which became the catalyst for the family's downfall. The curse instilled intense greed and paranoia, driving Fasold to murder his own father, Hreiðmarr, to possess the entire treasure. This act of patricide marked a profound moral descent. Fasold then transformed himself into a dragon, Fafnir, to guard the hoard, embodying the corrupting influence of wealth and the degeneration of a once-formidable giant into a monstrous beast. This transformation is not merely physical; it represents a spiritual and moral decay, driven by avarice and fueled by the curse attached to Andvari's gold. The transformation into a dragon is a common motif in Germanic mythology, often symbolizing greed and the corruption of power.

extendedContent.title: The Guardian of the Hoard: Fasold's Greatest Challenge
extendedContent.content:
As Fafnir, the dragon, Fasold faced the challenge of guarding the immense hoard of gold, which became both his greatest treasure and his prison. His existence was reduced to a solitary vigilance, constantly tormented by the fear of losing his wealth. The dragon form amplified his inherent greed, making him increasingly suspicious and hostile. He became a terrifying presence in the Gnita-Heath, poisoning the land with his breath and instilling fear in all who dared approach. His challenge was not merely physical; it was a psychological battle against the corrosive effects of the curse and his own insatiable avarice.

The greatest threat to Fasold's hoard came in the form of Sigurd (Siegfried), the hero destined to slay him. Regin, Fasold's brother, manipulated Sigurd, forging the sword Gram for him and instructing him to kill the dragon and retrieve the treasure. The confrontation between Sigurd and Fafnir is a pivotal moment in the Volsunga Saga, representing the triumph of heroism and justice over greed and corruption. Although Fafnir possessed immense strength and poisonous breath, he was ultimately outmatched by Sigurd's courage, skill, and the magical sword Gram.

extendedContent.title: Death & Prophecy: The Fall of a Dragon
extendedContent.content:
The death of Fasold at the hands of Sigurd is a critical turning point in the Volsunga Saga. Guided by Regin, Sigurd dug a pit along Fafnir's usual path to water and ambushed the dragon, stabbing him in the underbelly with Gram. As Fafnir lay dying, he engaged in a brief but significant dialogue with Sigurd, revealing fragments of his past, the curse of the Andvari's ring, and foretelling Sigurd's own tragic fate. These dying words highlight the interconnectedness of fate and the devastating consequences of greed and betrayal.

Fasold's death serves as a symbolic cleansing of the poisoned land. The dragon's blood, though poisonous, is also said to have granted Sigurd invulnerability (with the exception of a leaf-shaped spot on his back). This act of slaying the dragon and acquiring the hoard sets in motion a new cycle of events, leading to further conflicts and tragedies within the Volsung clan. The acquisition of the hoard, initially intended to bring wealth and power, ultimately becomes a source of misfortune, fulfilling the prophecy of the curse.

extendedContent.title: Legacy & Symbolic Significance: Greed and its Consequences
extendedContent.content:
Fasold's primary legacy lies in his role as a cautionary figure against the dangers of greed and the corrupting influence of wealth. His transformation into a dragon serves as a potent symbol of the dehumanizing effects of avarice, demonstrating how the pursuit of material possessions can lead to moral degradation and ultimately, destruction. The Volsunga Saga and related narratives consistently portray the negative consequences of unchecked desire and the importance of ethical conduct, even in the face of temptation.

Fasold's story resonates across various interpretations. Some view him as a representation of the primal forces of nature, tamed and defeated by the forces of civilization embodied by Sigurd. Others see him as a tragic figure, a victim of circumstance and the curse that plagues his family. Regardless of interpretation, Fasold's character serves as a powerful reminder of the destructive potential of greed and the importance of moral integrity in the face of temptation. His story continues to be relevant in modern society, where the pursuit of wealth often overshadows ethical considerations.

shortDescription: Fasold, a giant in Norse myth, is known for his greed and role as Fafnir's father. His demand for wergild sets the stage for Sigurd's dragon-slaying.
